name: AFJOC (African Joint Operation Against Cybercrime)
description: INTERPOL-led joint operation programme providing African law enforcement agencies with cybercrime investigation equipment, blockchain analysis software, and digital forensic capabilities. Has distributed tools to 36 African countries including Ghana.
output: Technical assistance, tool access, joint investigation support
legalNotes: Law enforcement only via INTERPOL National Central Bureau.

name: AFRIPOL Cybercrime Reporting
description: African Union's police cooperation mechanism for cybercrime. Coordinates joint operations across 55 AU member states including child exploitation cases. Works with INTERPOL's AFJOC programme to share intelligence and deploy forensic tools across Africa.
input: Case referral via national police liaison
output: Cross-border intelligence sharing, joint investigation support
legalNotes: Access via national police AFRIPOL liaison officer. Not for direct public use.

name: CAID (Child Abuse Image Database)
description: UK national CSAM hash database created in 2013 by the UK NCA and Home Office. Allows UK police forces to automatically scan seized devices against 20 million+ known CSAM hashes, dramatically reducing review time. Integrated with major forensic tools.
input: Hash values from seized device forensics
output: Match results against national CSAM hash database
legalNotes: UK law enforcement only. Access via UK NCA or regional police forces. Not available internationally directly — cross-reference via INTERPOL ICSE.
evidenceAdmissibility: CAID matches are court-admissible in UK proceedings.

name: CESIUM
description: AI-enabled child safeguarding system developed by Trilateral Research. Integrates multi-agency data to improve early identification of children at risk of exploitation and provides near real-time decision support for child protection officers. Designed for statutory agencies across EU jurisdictions.
input: Multi-agency case records, risk assessment data
output: Risk scores, early warning alerts, recommended interventions, decision support reports
legalNotes: Restricted to statutory child protection agencies and law enforcement. GDPR-compliant. Data processing agreement required. EU jurisdiction focus.
reliabilityNote: AI risk scores are decision-support tools, not automated decisions. Human review is mandatory before any action. Algorithm subject to bias audit requirements under EU AI Act.
evidenceAdmissibility: Decision support product — not a source of evidence in itself.

name: Europol IOCTA Reporting Platform
description: Europol's platform for reporting cybercrime including online child sexual exploitation. Enables cross-border case referrals and intelligence sharing between EU member states and partner countries for CSAM and online grooming investigations.
output: Cross-border referral, intelligence sharing with EU LE agencies
legalNotes: Public reporting open. Law enforcement cross-border referrals via official Europol channels.
evidenceAdmissibility: Intelligence product — use to initiate investigations.

name: F1 Video Hash Tool
description: Donated to ICMEC by Friend MTS in 2014. Generates unique hashes of child exploitation videos — including partial copies — so investigators can quickly identify CSAM within seized material without re-reviewing every file manually.
output: Hash fingerprint for comparison against known CSAM video database
legalNotes: Distributed by ICMEC to law enforcement only. Contact ICMEC to request access.

name: Ghana Police Cyber Crime Unit
description: Ghana Police Service Cyber Crime Unit with a dedicated Child Protection Digital Forensics Laboratory. Investigates online child exploitation and abuse. Supported by UNICEF and NCMEC. Decentralised across all 25 regional police commands in Ghana.
output: Investigation initiation, digital forensics support
legalNotes: Ghana jurisdiction. For cross-border cases coordinate via INTERPOL NCB Accra.

name: Griffeye Analyze DI
description: Donated to ICMEC by Safer Society Group in 2015. A digital media investigation platform designed to help investigators review, categorise and manage large volumes of seized images and videos. Reduces time spent reviewing non-CSAM material.
output: Categorised media library with flagged content
legalNotes: Law enforcement only. Distributed through ICMEC. Contact ICMEC for a free license.
evidenceAdmissibility: Widely used by law enforcement globally. Results are court-admissible.

name: INHOPE ICCAM
description: INHOPE's Image Content Analysis and Management platform. Used by 50+ member hotlines globally to receive, analyze, and exchange CSAM reports across jurisdictions. Integrates PhotoDNA hash matching to avoid re-review of known content. Enables rapid cross-border referral to national law enforcement agencies.
input: CSAM report URL or image hash
output: Content classification, hash match result, cross-border referral to LE and hosting providers
legalNotes: Access restricted to INHOPE member hotlines and approved partner organisations. Reports forwarded to national law enforcement automatically upon classification.
reliabilityNote: Classification is performed by trained hotline analysts. PhotoDNA hashes are industry-standard — high reliability for known CSAM.
evidenceAdmissibility: INHOPE classifications and referral logs are used in international prosecutions.

name: INTERPOL ICSE Database
description: INTERPOL's International Child Sexual Exploitation database holds 4.9 million images/videos and has helped identify 42,300 victims worldwide. Uses image/video comparison, audio analysis, facial recognition and metadata to link victims, abusers and locations across 70+ countries.
output: Cross-border victim/abuser/location matches, case linkages
legalNotes: Restricted to law enforcement from INTERPOL member countries only. Access via national INTERPOL NCB (National Central Bureau).
evidenceAdmissibility: INTERPOL match results are used in international prosecutions.

name: IWF Reporting Portal
description: The Internet Watch Foundation's portal for reporting child sexual abuse material online. IWF analysts review reports and issue takedown notices to hosting providers globally. Law enforcement can access the IWF hash list for known CSAM URLs.
output: Confirmed classification, takedown request to hosting provider
legalNotes: Public reporting available. IWF hash list API available to approved companies and law enforcement.
evidenceAdmissibility: IWF classification used as evidence in UK and international cases.

name: NCMEC CyberTipline
description: The US national reporting mechanism for online child sexual exploitation. Electronic Service Providers (ESPs) are legally required to report CSAM to the CyberTipline. Law enforcement can query tip data and receive reports linked to their jurisdiction.
input: Report submission or law enforcement query
output: ESP reports with IP data, account info, images hashes
legalNotes: Public reporting is open. Law enforcement access requires NCMEC LEA portal account. Reports forwarded internationally via INTERPOL/Europol partnerships.
evidenceAdmissibility: CyberTipline reports are used as evidence in prosecutions globally.

name: PhotoDNA
description: Developed by Microsoft with Dartmouth College, PhotoDNA compiles a digital signature (hash) of images that can be matched against a database of known CSAM. Industry standard for detecting known child abuse imagery across platforms. Free for law enforcement.
output: Hash match result against known CSAM database
legalNotes: For law enforcement and approved NGOs only. Requires Microsoft approval. No actual CSAM images are shared — only hash values.
evidenceAdmissibility: Hash match results are widely accepted as evidence in court proceedings.

name: Project VIC
description: A global collaborative network enabling law enforcement agencies to share CSAM hash sets and victim identification data in real-time. Integrates with major forensic tools (Cellebrite, Magnet, Griffeye) to automatically flag known CSAM during device analysis.
output: Match results with victim/case categorisation
legalNotes: Law enforcement only. Requires agency registration with Project VIC International.

name: ReportBox 2.0
description: INHOPE's next-generation reporting and exchange platform enabling global hotlines to rapidly process, classify, and share CSAM records across member organisations and coordinate simultaneous takedowns with hosting providers across multiple jurisdictions.
output: Coordinated cross-border takedown notices, case exchange records
legalNotes: Restricted to INHOPE member hotlines. Coordinated takedowns require engagement with national legal processes in each hosting jurisdiction.
reliabilityNote: Relies on member hotline analyst review for classification accuracy.
evidenceAdmissibility: Exchange records and takedown logs are used as supporting evidence in prosecution.

name: Thorn Spotlight
description: Free tool for law enforcement that helps identify and recover child sex trafficking victims. Scans millions of online sex ads to surface likely victim profiles, prioritising cases with minors. Used by 8,000+ law enforcement officers across 40 countries.
output: Linked escort/trafficking ads, victim profiles, location patterns
legalNotes: Law enforcement only. Free registration at thorn.org. Available in 40+ countries.
evidenceAdmissibility: Ad data used as investigative leads — corroborate with traditional evidence.

name: Arkham Intelligence

legalNotes: Free tier available. Paid plans for advanced features.

name: Blockchair

output: Transaction history, wallet balance, linked addresses
legalNotes: Public blockchain data. No warrant required for on-chain data. Evidence should be corroborated.

name: Chainalysis Reactor

output: Entity attribution, fund flow graph, court-ready reports
legalNotes: Enterprise pricing ~$40,000+/year. Law enforcement agencies may qualify for subsidised access. Contact Chainalysis public sector team.
evidenceAdmissibility: Chainalysis reports are accepted as evidence in courts globally.
